claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! I'm here to help you check your eligibility for Child Benefit. Let me ask you some questions to get started.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for:**

How old is your child? Are they:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please also let me know:
- How many children you're asking about
- Their names (so I can keep track if there are multiple children)
- Their ages

This will help me guide you through the right eligibility checks for your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for your help. I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 8 years old. I don't live with him at the moment, but I do help out financially where I can.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you're asking about your son **Alex**, who is **8 years old**.

Since Alex is under 16, that's a good start. Now I need to ask you a few important questions to determine your eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Since Alex is 8 years old, he wouldn't be working full-time, so that's fine.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Since Alex is 8, he wouldn't be receiving benefits like Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in his own right, so that's also fine.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Alex is 8, so he's not in any apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, I need to ask about **your residency status**:

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

If not, do any of these situations apply to you:
- You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
- You have pre-settled status and are working/studying/job seeking
- You're living abroad as a Crown servant

To keep it simple - do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Since you normally live in the UK, that meets the residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, this is an important question. You mentioned you don't live with Alex at the moment, but you do help out financially.

**To be considered "responsible" for Alex, you need to meet one of these criteria:**

1. **Living with the child** - You live with Alex (which you've said you don't currently)
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** - You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care. This can include things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing costs, presents - either as weekly/monthly payments or lump sums.
3. **Paying maintenance** - Under a court order or formal agreement

Let me get the current Child Benefit amounts for you:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The current Child Benefit rate is approximately **£25.60 per week** for the eldest/only child (though you should check gov.uk for the exact current rate).

**So, my question to you is:**

Do you contribute **at least £25.60 per week** (or about £110 per month) towards Alex's upkeep? This can include:
- Money you give for his care
- Buying food, clothes, or other essentials
- Pocket money
- Presents and other support

Or, do you pay maintenance under a court order or formal agreement?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Alex's care. I help out with things like clothes, shoes, and bits and pieces he needs. I'd say I'm giving around that amount each week to help with his upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful to know! Since you're contributing around £25.60 per week towards Alex's care through things like clothes, shoes, and other essentials, that does meet the requirement for being "responsible" for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, this is another important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example, is Alex's other parent or someone else who lives with him already receiving Child Benefit for him?

It's important to know because only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, that's good to know. Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, we can continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about Alex's living situation:

**Is Alex currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or living normally (with family/at home)?

What is Alex's current living situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living normally - he's with his mum at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect. Since Alex is living normally with his mum, let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
One final question:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the council financially supporting where Alex lives or his day-to-day care costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## ✅ Great news! You ARE eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

**Summary:**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for your 8-year-old son Alex. Even though you don't live with him, you've established **responsibility** for him by contributing at least the required amount (around £25.60 per week) towards his upkeep through clothes, shoes, and other essentials he needs.

Here's why you qualify:
- ✅ Alex is 8 years old (under 16)
- ✅ You normally live in the UK
- ✅ You're responsible for Alex through financial contributions to his care
- ✅ No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for him
- ✅ Alex is living normally (not in hospital, care, or being fostered)
- ✅ The council isn't paying for his accommodation or maintenance

---

## 📋 Next Steps - How to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate

**Important note about the High Income Child Benefit Charge:**
- If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you'll need to pay a tax charge that increases until it reaches 100% at £80,000
- This doesn't affect your *eligibility* - you can still claim, but you may have to pay some or all of it back through your tax return
- Some people in this situation choose not to claim the payments but still register to protect their National Insurance credits

**Payment details:**
- Payments are typically made every 4 weeks
- The current rate is approximately **£25.60 per week** for an only or eldest child

---

**You can apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), not working full-time, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant normally lives in the UK (meeting residency requirements) and is responsible for Alex by contributing at least £25.60 per week towards his upkeep through clothes, shoes, and other essentials. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is living normally with his mother, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 8-year-old son Alex. Despite not living with Alex, the claimant contributes financially to his upkeep at the required level, establishing responsibility. All other criteria including UK residency, no other claimant, and normal living arrangements are satisfied.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is living normally with his mother, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.
